---
title: "PS7: Joins, Pivots, and Strings"
subtitle: "STAT 133"
author: "Yuan Xie"
format: 
  html:
    toc: true
    number-sections: true
    theme: simplex
embed-resources: true
---

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = TRUE, error = TRUE)
library(tidyverse)
```

::: {.callout-note icon=false}

## General Instructions

-   Download the template file `ps7.qmd` to answer the questions.

-   Rename `ps7.qmd` to `ps7-first-last.qmd`, where `first` and `last`
    are your first and last names (e.g. `ps7-gaston-sanchez.qmd`).

-   Render your qmd file to an HTML file and submit both your qmd and HTML files to the corresponding assignment submission in bCourses.

:::

```{r}
#| echo: false
# This cell is to create the dataframes you will be 
# working with in the questions. Do not change the
# contents of this cell.
planes <- data.frame(
  tailnum = c("N8907A","N77296","N456AA","N821AY","N508MJ",
              "N250WN","N589AS","N787UA","N39418","N725UW"),
  
  year = c(2004,2005,1988,2005,2003,2006,2007,1997,2008,1999),
    
  manufacturer = c("BOMBARDIER INC","BOEING","MCDONNELL DOUGLAS","BOMBARDIER INC",
                   "BOMBARDIER INC","BOEING","BOEING","BOEING","BOEING","AIRBUS INDUSTRIE"),
  
  model = c("CL-600-2B19","737-824","DC-9-82(MD-82)","CL-600-2B19",
            "CL-600-2C10","737-7H4","737-890","777-222","737-924ER","A319-112"),
  
  engines = c(2,2,3,2,1,2,2,2,2,2),
  
  seats = c(55,149,172,55,80,140,149,400,191,179),
      
  stringsAsFactors = FALSE
)

flights <- data.frame(
  time_hour = as.POSIXct(c(
    "2013-10-25 20:00:00",
    "2013-02-25 13:00:00",
    "2013-05-19 15:00:00",
    "2013-06-13 13:00:00",
    "2013-03-31 08:00:00",
    "2013-03-20 11:00:00",
    "2013-04-27 19:00:00",
    "2013-03-04 09:00:00",
    "2013-08-01 13:00:00",
    "2013-08-06 06:00:00"
  )),
  
  flight = c(1049, 4270, 1636, 331, 3635,
             1143, 3798, 3739, 1601, 1701),
  
  tailnum = c("N77296","N8907A","N77296","N456AA","N821AY",
              "N39418","N821AY","N8907A","N39418","N39418"),
  
  origin = c("EWR","EWR","EWR","LGA","JFK",
             "EWR","JFK","JFK","EWR","EWR"),
  
  dep_delay = c(10, -15, 17, 74, -5,
                3, 57, -7, -1, -2),
  
  stringsAsFactors = FALSE
)

airports <- data.frame(
  faa = c("ICT","BGM","KLN","INW","HOP",
          "EWR","IRK","LGA","JFK","2J9"),
  
  name = c("Wichita Mid Continent",
           "Greater Binghamton Edwin A Link Fld",
           "Larsen Bay Airport",
           "Winslow-Lindbergh Regional Airport",
           "Campbell Aaf",
           "Newark Liberty Intl",
           "Kirksville Regional Airport",
           "La Guardia",
           "John F Kennedy Intl",
           "Quincy Municipal Airport"),
  
  lat = c(37.6, 42.2, 57.5, 35.0, 36.7,
          40.7, 40.1, 40.8, 40.6, 30.6),
  
  lon = c(-97.4, -76.0, -154.0, -111.0, -87.5,
          -74.2, -92.5, -73.9, -73.8, -84.6),
  
  tzone = c("America/Chicago",
            "America/New_York",
            "America/Anchorage",
            "America/Phoenix",
            "America/Chicago",
            "America/New_York",
            "America/Chicago",
            "America/New_York",
            "America/New_York",
            "America/New_York"),
  
  stringsAsFactors = FALSE
)
```

# Joins with Airplane Data

To give you practice doing joins, we are going to be using subsets of data from the package `nycflights13`. You don't need to install this package. All of the dataframes have been created in the code cell above that is hidden in the html document. The dataframes are called `flights`, `planes`, and `airports`. You can look at them below.

```{r}
flights
```

```{r}
planes
```

```{r}
airports
```

## Identifying Keys

Recall that in order to join two tables, the tables must have a common __key__. A column that we join the tables `by`.


a) Can we join the `flights` dataframe with the `planes` dataframe? If so, what is the common key?

::: {.callout-note}
# Type Your Answer in this Callout Block

Yes. We can join the `flights` dataframe with the `planes` dataframe. The common key is `tailnum`.

:::
\


b) Can we join the `flights` dataframe with the `airports` dataframe? If so, what is the common key?

::: {.callout-note}
# Type Your Answer in this Callout Block

Yes. The tables can be joined because `flights$origin` and `airports$faa` both means the airport code. We can set the key as `by = c("origin" = "faa")`.

:::
\

c) Can we join the `airports` dataframe with the `planes` dataframe? If so, what is the common key?

::: {.callout-note}
# Type Your Answer in this Callout Block

No, because there is no common variables in the two dataframes, so there is no common key.

:::
\

## Left/Right Joins

Recall that a `left_join()` tries to match every entry in the left table with an entry in the right table. For each entry in the left table that has no entry in the right, it fills it in with a `NA`. If you need a visual, please refer to the lecture slides: [Lecture 17: Joins and Pivots](https://stat133.berkeley.edu/spring-2026/lectures/17-joins-pivots/slides.html#/section-4)

a) Use `left_join()` to join `planes` with `flights`.

```{r}
left_join(planes, flights, by = "tailnum")
```

\

b) Copy your code from (a) and change it to `right_join()`.

```{r}
right_join(planes, flights, by = "tailnum")
```

\

What is different about the two tables that were generated in (a) and (b)? Why does one contain missing values and the other doesn't? Type your answer in the callout block below.

::: {.callout-note}
# Type Your Answer in this Callout Block

`left_join()` keeps all rows of the `planes` dataframe, while `right_join()` keeps all rows of the `flights` dataframe. `NA` appears when using `left_join()` because some planes do not have corresponding flights.

:::

\

c) Use `left_join()` to generate the same table from part (b). It's ok that the columns may not be in the same order.

```{r}
left_join(flights, planes, by = "tailnum")
```

\

## Inner/Full Joins

Recall that an `inner_join()` only returns a table with entries that match the key in both tables. While an outer join returns a table containing all entries of both tables, using `NA` to fill in missing information. If you need a visual, please refer to the lecture slides: [Lecture 17: Joins and Pivots](https://stat133.berkeley.edu/spring-2026/lectures/17-joins-pivots/slides.html#/section-4)

a) Use `inner_join()` to join `flights` with `airports`.

```{r}
inner_join(flights, airports, by = c("origin" = "faa"))
```

\

b) Use `inner_join()` to join `flights` with `planes`.

```{r}
inner_join(flights, planes, by = "tailnum")
```

\

c) Use `inner_join()` to make a table containing values from `flights`, `airports`, and `planes`.

```{r}
flights %>%
  inner_join(airports, by = c("origin" = "faa")) %>%
  inner_join(planes, by = "tailnum")
```

\

d) Join the three tables together again, but with `full_join()` instead of `inner_join()`.

```{r}
flights %>%
  full_join(airports, by = c("origin" = "faa")) %>%
  full_join(planes, by = "tailnum")
```

Why are there so many `NA` values in the table from (d)? Choose a row with `NA` values in it and explain why it contains `NA`.

::: {.callout-note}
# Type Your Answer in this Callout Block

`full_join()` combines all rows and all columns these data frames. Where there are not matching values, returns `NA` for the ones missing. For example, row 11 from the `airports` dataframe does not have a corresponding flight in the `flights` dataframe, so the flight-related columns are filled with `NA`.

:::

e) Why might we want to do a `full_join()` over an `inner_join()` if the result might have many missing values in the table?

::: {.callout-note}
# Type Your Answer in this Callout Block

`full_join()` keeps all rows. This helps us to see all available data and identify where information is missing. We will not lose any useful data.

:::

## Anti Joins

Anti joins are used to return only values that are exclusive to the left table. If you need a visual, please refer to the lecture slides: [Lecture 17: Joins and Pivots](https://stat133.berkeley.edu/spring-2026/lectures/17-joins-pivots/slides.html#/section-4)

a) Use `anti_join()` to return a table of airports that do not appear in the `flights` table.

```{r}
anti_join(airports, flights, by = c("faa"= "origin"))
```

\

b) Use `anti_join()` and `count()` to get a table that shows the number of airplanes from each manufacturer for which we don't have `flight` data.

```{r}
planes %>%
  anti_join(flights, by = "tailnum") %>%
  count(manufacturer)
```

\

# Pivots

## `pivot_longer()` with `world_bank_pop`

`tidyverse` comes with the tibble `world_bank_pop`, we're going to be using a subset of that data to practice pivots.

```{r}
bank_pop_slice <-  world_bank_pop |> 
    select("country", "indicator", "2005", "2006", "2007") |>
    slice_head(n = 10)
head(bank_pop_slice)
```

a) Use `pivot_longer()` to reshape the data `bank_pop_slice` into long format, with a new column `year` and the values stored in the original three columns placed in a table with a name of your choosing.

```{r}
pivot_longer(data = bank_pop_slice, 
             cols = c("2005", "2006", "2007"), 
             names_to = "year", 
             values_to = "population")
```

Why does it not make sense to reformat the data in this manner?

::: {.callout-note}
# Type Your Answer in this Callout Block

It does not make sense because the indicators are different. Combining them in one column may lead to confusion.

:::

\

b) Filter `bank_pop_slice` to only contain `indicator == "SP.URB.TOTL"`, then convert the data to long format using `values_to = "pop"` and only keep the `country`, `year`, and `pop`.

```{r}
bank_pop_slice %>%
  filter(indicator == "SP.URB.TOTL") %>%
  pivot_longer(
    cols = c("2005", "2006", "2007"),
    names_to = "year",
    values_to = "pop"
  ) %>%
  select(country, year, pop)
```

## `pivot_wider()` with `fish_encounters`

The dataset `fish_encounters` that comes with `tidyr` records when fish were seen by automatic monitoring stations.

```{r}
head(fish_encounters)
```

a) Use `pivot_wider()` to convert the table into wide format, with a column for each monitoring station.

```{r}
pivot_wider(data = fish_encounters,
            names_from = "station",
            values_from = "seen")
```

b) Since some fish were not seen at certain stations, there are a lot of missing values in the previous table. Run the same code, but add `values_fill = 0` to replace all missing values `NA` with `0` in the wide table.

```{r}
pivot_wider(data = fish_encounters,
            names_from = "station",
            values_from = "seen",
            values_fill = 0)
```

# Regular Expressions with `"stringr"`

Consider the following character with the names of some animals:

```{r echo=TRUE}
animals = c(
  'dog', 'cat', 'bird', 'dolphin', 'lion', 'zebra', 'tiger', 
  'wolf', 'whale', 'eagle', 'pig', 'osprey', 'kangaroo', 'koala')
```

Let's match the pattern `dog` with `str_match()`

```{r echo=TRUE}
str_match(animals, pattern = 'dog')
```

As you can tell, the output of `str_match()` is an array with as many rows as
elements in the input vector. If there is a match, the matched string pattern 
is displayed. If there is no match, then you get a missing value `NA`

You can use `str_detect()` to check if there is a match:

```{r echo=TRUE}
str_detect(animals, 'dog')
```


To _extract_ the matched pattern, you can use `str_extract()`:

```{r echo=TRUE}
str_extract(animals, pattern = 'dog')
```

Sometimes you may want to extract the elements of the input vector associated to
a match. For instance, say we are interested in those animals that contain the
letter `"a"`. We can use the output of `str_detect()` to do logical subsetting

```{r}
animals[str_detect(animals, 'a')]
```

## Subsetting with `str_detect()`

Use logical subsetting with `str_detect()`, to find the names of animals with:

a) zero or more `o` 

```{r}
animals[str_detect(animals, 'o*')]
```


b) zero or one `o`

```{r}
animals[str_detect(animals, 'o?')]
```


c) at least 1 `o`: `"dog" "dolphin" "lion" "wolf" "osprey" "kangaroo" "koala"`

```{r}
animals[str_detect(animals, 'o+')]
```


d) exactly 2 `o`'s together: `"kangaroo"`

```{r}
animals[str_detect(animals, 'oo')]
```


e) one `o`, but not two `o`'s together: `"dog" "dolphin" "lion" "wolf" "osprey" "koala"`

```{r}
animals[str_detect(animals, "o") & !str_detect(animals, "oo")]
```


f) two vowels together: `"lion" "eagle" "kangaroo" "koala"`

```{r}
animals[str_detect(animals, '(a|e|i|o|u)(a|e|i|o|u)')]
```


g) two or more consonants together: `"bird" "dolphin"  "zebra" "wolf" "whale" "eagle" "osprey" "kangaroo"`

```{r}
animals[str_detect(animals, '[^aeiou]{2,}')]
```


h) three consonants together: `"dolphin" "osprey"`

```{r}
animals[str_detect(animals, "[^aeiou]{3}")]
```


i) three letters only: `"dog" "cat" "pig"`

```{r}
animals[str_detect(animals, "^\\w{3}$")]
```


j) four letters only: `"bird" "lion" "wolf"`

```{r}
animals[str_detect(animals, "^\\w{4}$")]
```


## File Names

Here's another character vector with some file names and their extensions:

```{r}
files <- c(
  'sales1.csv', 'orders.csv', 'sales2.csv',
  'sales3.csv', 'europe.csv', 'usa.csv', 'mex.csv',
  'CA.csv', 'FL.csv', 'NY.csv', 'TX.csv',
  'sales-europe.csv', 'sales-usa.csv', 'sales-mex.csv')
```


## Your turn

a) Find the file names containing numbers

```{r}
files[str_detect(files, "\\d")]
```


b) Find the file names containing no numbers

```{r}
files[!str_detect(files, "\\d")]
```


c) Find the file names containing lower case letters (including file extension)

```{r}
files[str_detect(files, "[[:lower:]]")]
```


d) Find the file names containing lower case letters (just the name, not the file extension)

```{r}
files[str_detect(files, "[[:lower:]]+\\.csv$")]
```


e) Find the file names containing a dash

```{r}
files[str_detect(files, "\\-")]
```


f) Find the file names containing no dash

```{r}
files[!str_detect(files, "\\-")]
```


g) Create a vector of files by replacing the 'csv' extension into 'txt' extension

```{r}
newfiles <- str_replace_all(files, "csv", "txt")
newfiles
```


h) Extract just the file name (without the extension)

```{r}
str_sub(files, start = 1, end = -5)
str_remove(files, "\\.csv$")
```
